question:
A collector of antique grandfather clocks sold at auction believes that the price received for the clocks depends on both the age of the clocks and the number of bidders at the auction. Thus, he hypothesizes the first-order model

$$
y= eta_0+ eta_1 x_1+ eta_2 x_2+\varepsilon
$$

where

$$
 egin{aligned}
& y=	ext { Auction price (dollars) } \
& x_1=	ext { Age of clock (years) } \
& x_2=	ext { Number of bidders }
\end{aligned}
$$


A sample of 32 auction prices of grandfather clocks, along with their age and the number of bidders, is given in Table 12.1.
a. Use scatterplots to plot the sample data. Interpret the plots.
b. Use the method of least squares to estimate the unknown parameters $ eta_0,  eta_1$, and $ eta_2$ of the model.
answer:
a. Minitab side-by-side scatterplots for examining the bivariate relationships between $y$ and $x_1$, and between $y$ and $x_2$, are shown in Figure 12.1. Of the two variables, age ($x_1$) appears to have the stronger linear relationship with auction price ($y$).

b. The model hypothesized is fit to the data of Table 12.1 with Minitab. A portion of the printout is reproduced in Figure 12.2. The least squares estimates of the $ eta$ parameters (highlighted) are $\hat{ eta}_0 = -1339$, $\hat{ eta}_1 = 12.74$, and $\hat{ eta}_2 = 85.95$. Therefore, the equation that minimizes SSE for this data set (i.e., the least squares prediction equation) is
\[
\hat{y} = -1339 + 12.74x_1 + 85.95x_2
\]

c. The minimum value of the sum of the squared errors, also highlighted in Figure 12.2, is $	ext{SSE} = 516727$.

d. Recall that the estimator of $\sigma^2$ for the straight-line model is $s^2 = 	ext{SSE}/(n - 2)$, and note that the denominator is ($n - 	ext{number of estimated }  eta$ parameters), which is ($n - 2$) in the straight-line model. Because we must estimate the three parameters $ eta_0,  eta_1,$ and $ eta_2$ for the first-order model, the estimator of $\sigma^2$ is
\[
s^2 =  rac{	ext{SSE}}{n - 3} =  rac{516727}{32 - 3} =  rac{516727}{29} = 17,818
\]
This value, often called mean square for error (MSE), is also highlighted at the bottom of the Minitab printout in Figure 12.2. The estimate of $\sigma$, then, is
\[
s = \sqrt{17,818} = 133.5
\]
which is highlighted in the middle of the Minitab printout in Figure 12.2. One useful interpretation of the estimated standard deviation $s$ is that the interval $\pm 2s$ will provide a rough approximation to the accuracy with which the model will predict future values of $y$ for given values of $x$. Thus, we expect the model to provide predictions of auction price to within about
\[
\pm 2s = \pm 2(133.5) = \pm 267 	ext{ dollars.}^*
\]

meta_info.caption: Fitting a First-Order Model: Price of an Antique Clock
meta_info.section: 12.2 Estimating and Making Inferences about the $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ Parameters
